core_identity.title: Probability from Venn Diagrams: Playing Cards Edition
core_identity.author: Gerry Docherty

curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: probabilities from Venn diagrams (addition rule, union and intersection)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Use Venn diagrams to model overlapping events and apply the addition rule P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B) accurately, avoiding double-counting.

verse_ality.driving_question: How do Venn diagrams help us calculate probabilities when events overlap, and why must we subtract the intersection?

knowing_world.focus: Model events with a Venn diagram using a 52-card deck and apply the addition rule for probability, interpreting union and intersection in context.
key_concepts: Addition rule: P(A ∪ B) = P(A) + P(B) − P(A ∩ B)
Complement and outside region (neither A nor B)
tools: Interactive: Venn Diagram Probability Game – Playing Cards Edition (canva_embed): https://www.canva.com/design/DAG6dFY0wQU/kTbV8ZNGGwf0v07jF6j9HA/view?embed

human_story.focus: Understand why overlaps matter when interpreting real data: people can belong to more than one group, and misreading intersections can lead to double-counting and unfair decisions.
justice_prompts: Where in your life might groups overlap? Why is it important to notice the intersection?
How could misinterpreting overlaps lead to flawed policies or unfair decisions?

expressive_self.focus: Use story and personal examples to make set membership and overlap feel meaningful, linking intersection to belonging, visibility, and fairness.
activities: Create a visual story of a card’s journey: imagine you are the King of Spades—what does it feel like to belong to both sets? What is it like to be outside both?
Sketch your own Venn diagram using two groups from your life (e.g., “likes music” and “plays a sport”) and identify the overlap.
In your own words: What does this lesson teach you about fairness, visibility, or belonging?

designed_world.focus: Use an interactive sorting game to classify all 52 cards into the four Venn regions, then calculate key probabilities from your completed diagram and test new set combinations.
design_challenges: Click “Deal Card” to reveal a card, then drag it into the correct region of the Venn diagram.
Sort into: A only (King but not black), B only (Black but not King), A ∩ B (Black Kings), Outside (neither King nor black).
After sorting all cards, calculate: P(King), P(Black), P(Black ∩ King), and P(Black ∪ King).
Use “Check Answers” for feedback and “Try Another” to explore new combinations (e.g., Queens and Red Cards).
Design prompt: How could this activity be redesigned for someone using a screen reader or voice controls?

regulation_supports: Sorting check-in: notice how your body feels as the diagram fills (calm, tense, rushed).
Breath pattern: inhale for “Place”, exhale for “Predict”.
When you feel confused about sets or overlaps, notice where you feel it and name one strategy that helps you get unstuck.
wellbeing_prompts: How does your body respond to sorting and decision-making tasks? What helps you stay steady when you’re uncertain?

pathways.name: Final Task: Probability from Venn Diagrams
pathways.focus: Complete the interactive Venn diagram sort and use your diagram to calculate union and intersection probabilities accurately, then reflect and extend by changing the sets.
products: A screenshot of your completed Venn diagram (all cards sorted).
Answers to the four probability questions: P(King), P(Black), P(Black ∩ King), P(Black ∪ King).
A short reflection: Which part of the Venn was hardest to fill? Why?
One idea to change the sets and test a new rule (e.g., Queens and Red Cards).
criteria: Conceptual Understanding: applies the addition rule correctly and interprets overlap as intersection.
Fluency & Accuracy: sorts cards correctly into all four regions and calculates probabilities accurately.
Application to Problems: uses the completed diagram to justify answers and avoid double-counting.
Independence & Reflection: reflects thoughtfully and suggests meaningful extensions or improvements.

required: Understanding of simple events and counting outcomes
Familiarity with playing cards (or quick introduction: suits/colours and face cards)

builds_toward: Probability with unions, intersections, and complements
Set notation in probability and algebraic reasoning
Data interpretation where categories overlap (avoiding double-counting)
